title: Inventory AI
tagline: Turn photos of stock and handwritten sheets into structured inventory records — without the classic OCR failure modes.

levels.label: Junior
levels.intro: Document capture and OCR pipeline: why 'take a photo and extract text' is harder than it looks.
levels.theory: OCR for inventory data fails in four predictable ways that don't appear in demo conditions: (1) Image quality gating — handwritten stock sheets captured in warehouse lighting with a phone camera produce blurry, skewed, unevenly-lit images. Standard OCR (Tesseract, Google Vision) degrades dramatically on these inputs. Pre-processing (deskew, denoise, contrast normalisation) is not optional. (2) Layout understanding — a handwritten stock sheet may have columns without visible gridlines, numbers written in margins, crossed-out items, and rows that span multiple lines. OCR that extracts text without understanding layout produces a stream of characters with no structural meaning. (3) Domain vocabulary — 'Chkn 350g' means 'Chicken 350g' in a butcher's stock sheet. A general language model doesn't know this. A domain-specific post-processing step that expands abbreviations is required. (4) Handwriting variability — '1' and '7' are visually identical in many handwriting styles; '0' and 'O' similarly. Quantity extraction from handwritten sheets must include downstream validation (does this quantity make sense for this product category?).
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Junior theory on image quality gating — specifically what pre-processing steps are required before OCR can be applied.

levels.label: Senior
levels.intro: Entity matching: fuzzy matching, canonical product names, and handling new products.
levels.theory: Once text is extracted from a stock sheet or photo, it must be matched to existing inventory records. This is an entity resolution problem, not a string matching problem. (1) Fuzzy matching — product names in handwritten sheets almost never exactly match canonical database names. 'Choc fudge cake 6" round' must match 'Chocolate Fudge Cake, 6 inch, Round' in your database. Levenshtein distance handles typos; token set ratio handles word-order variations; both fail on abbreviation-heavy inputs. A domain-tuned embedding model with approximate nearest-neighbour search is more robust. (2) Threshold setting — a match at 0.6 similarity may be correct or may be a completely different product. The threshold must be tuned per product category (short product names require higher thresholds than long ones). (3) New product handling — items that don't match any existing record at any confidence threshold must be flagged for human review, not silently discarded or silently added as new items. (4) Unit normalisation — '500g', '0.5kg', '1/2 kg' are the same quantity. Canonical unit normalisation must happen before matching.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Senior theory on fuzzy matching — specifically why Levenshtein distance alone fails on abbreviation-heavy handwritten inputs.

levels.label: Architect
levels.intro: Data integrity: double-counting, audit trails, rollback, and supplier data conflicts.
levels.theory: Inventory data errors are operationally expensive — over-counting leads to spoilage and cash tied up in excess stock; under-counting leads to stockouts. Architectural requirements for data integrity: (1) Idempotency — if the same sheet is processed twice (duplicate upload, retry after network failure), the inventory must not be double-counted. Processing must be keyed on document identity (hash of the image), and reprocessing the same document must produce an update, not an addition. (2) Confidence-gated updates — low-confidence extractions must not auto-update live inventory. They must enter a review queue where a human confirms or corrects before the inventory record changes. (3) Audit trail — every inventory change must record: what document caused it, what was extracted, what confidence level, who (human or system) approved the update, and when. This is required for stock reconciliation and loss investigation. (4) Conflict detection — if a phone photo says 'oranges: 12kg' and the supplier delivery note says 'oranges: 15kg', this is a discrepancy that must be flagged, not silently resolved by one source winning.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Architect theory on idempotency — specifically the consequences of processing the same document twice without deduplication.

levels.label: Executive
levels.intro: Operational fit: where AI OCR adds value vs creates risk, change management, and ROI calculation.
levels.theory: An AI inventory system is not automatically faster or more accurate than a trained human doing the same task. Executive decisions: (1) Error rate comparison — human data entry from handwritten sheets has a known error rate (typically 1-3% for trained staff). AI OCR on the same input has a different error rate profile: very low error rate on clean printed inputs, potentially higher error rate on degraded handwritten inputs depending on quality. The comparison must use the actual input distribution, not demo-quality images. (2) Exception handling cost — every low-confidence extraction that enters the human review queue costs human time. If 30% of extractions require review, the system's actual throughput is not 'fully automated'. Calculate the real automation rate on your actual input distribution. (3) Change management — staff who currently do manual data entry will be affected. The deployment plan must include training, a clear communication on how their role changes, and a transition period where the AI system is verified alongside rather than replacing the existing process. (4) Integration cost — connecting the inventory AI to the existing ERP or stock management system is frequently the most expensive and time-consuming part of deployment, and is often underestimated.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Executive theory on error rate comparison — specifically why demo-quality images produce misleading accuracy benchmarks.

tasks.scenario: You test your inventory OCR system on 100 handwritten stock sheets photographed in the office under good lighting. Accuracy is 94%. You deploy to 5 warehouses. Accuracy drops to 61%. What's the most likely cause?
options: The OCR model is overfitted specifically to office-style documents and simply needs to be retrained from scratch on a broader, more general mixture of document images before it can perform reliably anywhere else
Office-condition photos don't represent warehouse capture conditions — lighting, angle, and motion blur weren't in your test set, so 94% measured how good your conditions were, not the system

Your accuracy metric itself is the wrong one to be tracking here — 61% is actually a perfectly acceptable accuracy figure for handwritten inventory work once you account for typical industry benchmarks

The warehouses simply need better lighting installed — once the physical lighting conditions match what you tested under in the office, accuracy should return close to the 94% figure you originally measured
hints: Think about what conditions the test set represented and what conditions the deployment environment actually has. Re-read the Junior theory on image quality gating.
94% in controlled conditions tells you how the system performs on clean inputs. It tells you nothing about how it performs on the actual distribution of inputs your users produce.
tasks.why: Benchmark accuracy on cherry-picked or controlled inputs is not deployment accuracy. The representative test set must include: photos taken at angles, in varying warehouse lighting, with motion blur from handheld capture, on crumpled or stained paper. If you don't have these, your 94% figure is a measure of how good your test conditions were, not how good your system is. Always evaluate on a representative sample of actual deployment inputs before going live.

tasks.scenario: This pre-processing function passes raw phone camera images directly to OCR. Add the minimum pre-processing steps required for reliable handwritten document OCR.

checks.label: Handles image skew or perspective distortion

tasks.why: Raw phone camera images need at minimum three pre-processing steps before OCR: (1) Deskew/perspective correction — documents photographed at an angle produce trapezoidal distortion that degrades character recognition. OpenCV's findContours + warpPerspective handles this. (2) Denoising — camera sensor noise and paper texture create false edges. Gaussian or median filter removes high-frequency noise without destroying text edges. (3) Adaptive thresholding — converts to binary (black text on white background) while handling uneven illumination across the document. These three steps can take a 60% accurate OCR result to 85%+ on the same image.

tasks.scenario: Put the steps of an inventory OCR pipeline in the correct order.
items: Match extracted items to canonical inventory records using fuzzy matching
Capture or upload image of stock sheet or product
Run OCR to extract raw text and layout structure
Post-process: expand abbreviations, normalise units, validate quantities
tasks.why: Capture first. Pre-processing must happen before OCR — applying OCR to a skewed or noisy image then trying to fix the output is less effective than fixing the input first. OCR produces raw text. Post-processing (abbreviation expansion, unit normalisation, quantity validation) structures that text before matching. Matching is last because it requires clean, normalised data to produce reliable results.

tasks.scenario: Your OCR extracts '7' from a handwritten stock sheet. The item is listed as 'eggs'. Your database has 12 entries containing 'egg': eggs (dozen), eggs (6-pack), eggs (24), egg whites (carton), etc. How should the system handle this?
options: Auto-match to the highest-confidence database entry available — 'eggs (dozen)' at 0.82 similarity is the best match the system found, and acting on the system's best guess is the most efficient default behaviour
Flag the ambiguity explicitly: the item name is too short to match uniquely, quantity '7' is unusual for any egg format — require human confirmation before writing to inventory

Match to eggs (dozen) regardless, since 7 is reasonably close to 12 and rounding an ambiguous handwritten quantity to the nearest standard pack size is a sensible default for this kind of short, common item name

Reject the extraction entirely and discard it without any further action — when an input is this ambiguous, the safest response is simply to not process it at all rather than risk any kind of incorrect match
hints: Think about the downstream consequences of an incorrect auto-match in an inventory context. Re-read the Junior theory on handwriting variability and quantity validation.

An incorrect inventory update causes real operational problems: over or under-ordering, incorrect stock valuations. The cost of a human confirmation step is much lower than the cost of an incorrect auto-update.
tasks.why: Short item names combined with unusual quantities are high-ambiguity extraction situations. The system must recognise this pattern and route to human review rather than auto-matching. '7 eggs' doesn't match any standard stock unit cleanly — it might be a misread quantity (70? 17?), a custom batch, or a notation error. Human confirmation is required. Auto-matching ambiguous extractions to the 'best guess' database entry will systematically produce inventory errors.

tasks.scenario: Your fuzzy matching system uses Levenshtein distance to match 'choc fudge cake' to your product database. It returns 'Chocolate Cake (Standard)' at similarity 0.71 instead of 'Chocolate Fudge Cake 6inch Round' at similarity 0.68. The 0.68 match is the correct one. Why does Levenshtein fail here?
options: Levenshtein distance is working correctly here — 0.71 is mathematically greater than 0.68, so by definition the match with the higher similarity score is the better one to select between these two candidates

Levenshtein measures character edit distance, not meaning. 'Choc' to 'Chocolate' costs many edits even though they mean the same thing — abbreviation expansion or embeddings would outperform raw edit distance here
You need a larger product database overall — the matching is failing here specifically because there isn't enough product variety indexed yet for the algorithm to have more options to compare against

Lower the similarity threshold across the board to around 0.5 so that more candidate matches like this correct one are surfaced and considered, even if their raw similarity score looks comparatively low
hints: Think about what Levenshtein actually measures and what it misses. Re-read the Senior theory on fuzzy matching.

Levenshtein counts the minimum edits (insert, delete, substitute) to transform one string into another. 'choc' → 'Chocolate' requires 7 character operations. 'choc' → 'Choc' requires 1. The algorithm doesn't know 'choc' means 'chocolate'.
tasks.why: Levenshtein distance is a character-level metric. It treats 'choc' as unrelated to 'chocolate' because the edit distance is high. Domain embedding models trained on product name pairs learn that 'choc' == 'chocolate', 'fudge' is a discriminating token, and abbreviation patterns in this domain. Alternatively, abbreviation expansion as a pre-matching step (expand known domain abbreviations before matching) combined with token set ratio matching (match on word sets regardless of order) handles this class of problem better than raw Levenshtein.

tasks.scenario: This matching function auto-updates inventory for any match above 0.6 similarity. Fix it to route low-confidence matches to a human review queue instead of auto-updating.
tasks.starter_code:
def update_inventory(extracted_item: dict, db) -> None:
    matches = fuzzy_match(extracted_item['name'], db.products)
    best_match = max(matches, key=lambda x: x['score'])
    db.update_inventory(
        product_id=best_match['id'],
        quantity=extracted_item['quantity'],
        confidence=best_match['score']
    )

checks.label: Sets a confidence threshold above which auto-update applies
Routes below-threshold matches to a review queue

tasks.why: A 0.6 similarity threshold is too low for autonomous inventory updates. At 0.6, a different product in the same category can match. The correct pattern: auto-update only above a high-confidence threshold (typically 0.85-0.90, tuned per category). Between a lower threshold and the auto-update threshold, route to a human review queue with the extraction and proposed match displayed side-by-side. Below the lower threshold, create a 'new item' draft for human classification. Never silently discard non-matching items.

tasks.scenario: Write the unit normalisation rules for an inventory system processing input from UK hospitality businesses. Cover at minimum: weight units, volume units, and count/pack units — including common UK hospitality abbreviations.
tasks.placeholder:
Weight units: g, kg, oz, lb — normalise all to grams...
Volume units:...
tasks.why: Unit normalisation is a prerequisite for reliable quantity matching and stock valuation. Without it, '500g', '0.5kg', '1/2 kg', and 'half kilo' will create four separate database entries for the same quantity. UK hospitality has specific abbreviations: 'doz' (dozen), 'cs' (case), 'lt'/'ltr' (litre), 'ea' (each). The normalisation ruleset must cover these domain-specific forms, not just SI unit conversions.

tasks.scenario: The same image of a delivery note is uploaded twice — once by a warehouse manager, once by a team member who didn't know it had been processed. Your system processes it twice, doubles the inventory quantities, and your stock count is now wrong. What's the correct architectural fix?
options: Add a confirmation step before processing any upload, asking the user 'are you sure you want to process this document?' — a simple prompt like this is generally enough to prevent accidental double submissions
Hash the image content on upload and check against a processed-documents store before running extraction — if the hash exists, return the existing result and do not reprocess

Implement a flat 24-hour cooldown period between any uploads coming from the same source or user, so that a second upload of the same document within that window is automatically blocked from processing

Train your warehouse staff explicitly not to upload the same document more than once — addressing this through clear process guidance and team training is the most direct fix for this specific failure
hints: Think about how to make the processing operation safe to repeat regardless of user behaviour. Re-read the Architect theory on idempotency.
User behaviour cannot be the reliability mechanism. The system must be idempotent — processing the same document twice must produce the same result as processing it once, regardless of what users do.
tasks.why: Idempotency is achieved by keying processing on document identity. A cryptographic hash (SHA-256) of the image content is computed on upload. If that hash already exists in the processed-documents store, the system returns the previous result without rerunning extraction or updating inventory. This makes processing safe to retry and safe to duplicate — the result is always the same. User confirmation steps help but cannot be the primary defence against double-processing.

tasks.scenario: Your audit log records inventory changes but stores them in the same database as the live inventory data, with full update/delete permissions for the application user. Why is this insufficient for a regulatory audit trail?
options: It's sufficient as it stands — having any audit log in place, even one stored alongside the live operational data with full permissions, is meaningfully better than having no audit trail of changes at all
An audit log the application can update or delete isn't trustworthy — it can be altered after the fact. It must be append-only, ideally in a separate store with no application delete permission
The format being used is the actual problem here — audit logs specifically need to be stored as JSON documents rather than as rows in a relational table in order to count as a proper audit trail

The audit log simply needs to be backed up regularly to cloud storage in order to qualify as a proper audit trail — once backups are in place, the original mutability concern becomes largely irrelevant
hints: Think about what 'immutable' means for an audit record and whether your current storage architecture provides it. Re-read the Architect theory on audit trails.
If the same database user that writes inventory updates can also UPDATE or DELETE audit log records, the audit trail is not trustworthy — it can be altered after the fact.
tasks.why: An audit trail is only as trustworthy as its immutability. If the application database user can update or delete audit records (as it can in any standard relational setup with full table permissions), the trail can be altered. Correct approaches: write audit records to a separate, append-only store (AWS QLDB, a separate database with write-only permissions for the application, or an immutable object store); use database-level triggers that write to an append-only audit table; or write to an external audit service. The application must never be able to update or delete audit records.

tasks.scenario: This extraction function processes a document and updates inventory immediately. Add idempotency checking and a confidence gate that routes low-confidence results to a review queue.

checks.label: Computes a document identity hash for idempotency
Checks if this document has been processed before
Routes low-confidence extractions to review rather than auto-updating

tasks.why: Three changes are required: (1) compute a hash of the image before processing; (2) check that hash against a processed-documents store and return early if found; (3) after extraction, split items by confidence — high-confidence items update inventory directly, low-confidence items enter a review queue with the extracted data and proposed match displayed for human confirmation. The review queue must persist even if the process crashes (write to database, not in-memory).

tasks.scenario: Describe a conflict detection and resolution policy for the case where your OCR system extracts a quantity that conflicts with a supplier's electronic delivery note for the same product. Include: how the conflict is detected, what happens to the inventory record during conflict, and who resolves it.
tasks.placeholder: A conflict is detected when the OCR-extracted quantity for a product differs from...

checks.label: States that the inventory record is not auto-updated during conflict

tasks.why: When two sources disagree on a quantity (OCR says 12, delivery note says 15), the correct response is not to pick one automatically. Both could be right (delivery was short) or either could be wrong (OCR misread, delivery note has a data entry error). The inventory record should remain at its pre-update value while the conflict is flagged. Resolution requires a human to physically count or investigate. The audit trail must record: both claimed quantities, their sources, who resolved it, what the resolution was, and when.

tasks.scenario: You're adding a feature where staff can correct OCR errors via a mobile app. A staff member 'corrects' a quantity of 500kg of beef to 5000kg. Your system accepts this as a human-verified correction. What's the missing architectural control?
options: Nothing further is needed here — if a human staff member has personally verified a correction through the app, that verification step alone is sufficient grounds to treat the new quantity as correct

Human corrections need the same plausibility checks as AI extractions — a 10x quantity change on a perishable item is statistically anomalous and should require secondary confirmation regardless of source

Add a requirement that every correction submitted through the app must include a supporting photo — once a photo is attached as evidence, the correction can be accepted without any further validation step

Log the correction in the audit trail and accept it into the live inventory immediately — the log itself provides the accountability needed here even without any additional check on the size of the change
hints: Think about what validation applies to human data entry vs AI extraction. Re-read the Architect theory on confidence-gated updates.
Human data entry errors are well-documented and common. A system that accepts any human correction without plausibility checks will experience data quality issues from fat-finger errors alone.
tasks.why: Human corrections are not exempt from validation. A 10x quantity change for a perishable item is a plausibility failure that warrants secondary confirmation. Validation rules should apply to all inventory changes regardless of source: changes beyond a statistical threshold (e.g. >300% of typical delivery quantity) require secondary confirmation. This catches fat-finger errors (500 mistyped as 5000), malicious changes, and genuine confusion. The human reviewer workflow is: flag for review, display the before/after, require a secondary confirmation from a different staff member for large changes.

tasks.scenario: Your AI inventory system claims 94% accuracy on clean inputs. Your pilot in a busy restaurant kitchen shows staff spend 40 minutes per day reviewing and correcting AI extractions compared to 25 minutes doing manual data entry previously. What does this tell you about your deployment?
options: The pilot conditions observed so far were simply unusual for this kitchen — accuracy and review time will improve naturally as staff become more familiar with the system through continued day-to-day use, and teams that have taken this approach in similar situations have generally reported it as sufficient without needing further changes
94% on clean inputs hasn't translated to the actual input distribution. Review overhead exceeding the old manual process means this deployment is net negative — fix accuracy or don't deploy here

The staff members involved in the pilot need to take noticeably better photos when capturing stock sheets — this is fundamentally a staff training and change management issue rather than a system design one, which is also the path of least resistance given the current system design and the time constraints most teams are working under

40 minutes of review time per day is an acceptable cost here — the benefits of the system will become visible over time through reduced stock wastage, even if the daily time investment looks high right now, and teams that have taken this approach in similar situations have generally reported it as sufficient without needing further changes
hints: Calculate the net time saving (or cost) before concluding the deployment is successful. Re-read the Executive theory on error rate comparison and exception handling cost.
The system is creating more work than it's saving. This is not a training problem — it's a product-market fit problem in this specific deployment context.
tasks.why: The 40-minute review overhead exceeds the 25-minute manual process by 60%. The AI system is making this kitchen slower, not faster. Before concluding this is a training or change management problem, quantify the actual automation rate: what percentage of extractions require no review? If it's below 70%, the system does not provide a net benefit in this context. The deployment decision is: either fix the input quality problem (better lighting, capture guidance), fix the model accuracy on this input distribution, or don't deploy here and find a context where the AI actually outperforms the manual process.

tasks.scenario: Write a 30-day parallel running plan for deploying your inventory AI in a single restaurant location. The plan must include: what runs in parallel, how you measure whether to proceed, and what triggers a rollback decision.
tasks.placeholder: During the 30-day parallel running period, both the AI system and the existing manual process will...

checks.label: States that both processes run simultaneously

tasks.why: Parallel running is essential for any system that affects inventory accuracy. During the parallel period, both the AI and existing manual process produce outputs independently. At the end of each day, compare the results and measure discrepancy rate, review queue volume, and staff time. Define a success threshold before starting (e.g. discrepancy rate below 2%, review queue below 20% of items, net time saving positive). If these aren't met by day 30, rollback means continuing the manual process, not a failed deployment — it's a valid outcome.

tasks.scenario: Your inventory AI product is targeted at small independent restaurants. The total addressable market is large but the per-customer revenue is low (£50-100/month). You need 2,000 customers to cover development and operational costs. What's the primary customer acquisition challenge specific to this market?
options: 2,000 customers is too many to reasonably target through this channel — focus instead on a smaller number of larger enterprise restaurant chains where each individual contract is worth significantly more revenue
Small restaurants are peer-trust-driven buyers with low tech adoption and high owner time scarcity. The primary channel is other owners who've seen it work, not digital ads or cold outreach

The pricing being used is too low for this market — raising it to roughly £500 per month would let you focus on a smaller segment of customers who can clearly afford a fully supported implementation process

The overall market for this kind of product is simply too small to be commercially viable at the scale needed — expanding into other retail inventory categories beyond hospitality is the more promising path
hints: Think about how small business purchasing decisions are made and what trust signals drive them. Re-read the Executive theory on change management.
Small business owners are not buying software — they're buying proven solutions to known problems, from trusted sources.
tasks.why: Small independent restaurants are peer-trust-driven buyers. The primary acquisition motion is: (1) get a few early adopters who see genuine time savings; (2) make them visible advocates (case studies, referral programmes, word of mouth at supplier events); (3) design the product for low-friction trial (free trial with their own data, no setup consultant required). Digital marketing, cold outreach, and trade shows are expensive and slow in this market. Peer recommendation from a neighbouring restaurant owner is worth 10x any paid channel.

tasks.scenario: Your inventory AI uses a vision-language model that was updated by the model provider. After the update, extraction accuracy on handwritten inputs drops from 85% to 71%. You discover this 3 days after it happened — during which time inventory records have been wrong. What process failure does this reveal?
options: This is expected and not a cause for concern — model providers update their underlying models on a regular basis, and some fluctuation in extraction accuracy after an update is a normal part of using a third-party model
No accuracy monitoring was in place. A system dependent on a third-party model needs continuous monitoring against a golden test set so silent model regressions are caught in hours, not days

The model provider should have proactively warned you before pushing this particular update — the real fix here is simply to request that they notify you ahead of time before any future changes are deployed

Update your contract with the model provider to explicitly require advance notice of any future model updates — once that clause is in place, this specific kind of accuracy regression won't be able to recur
hints: Think about how quickly a 14-point accuracy drop would be detectable without monitoring, and what three days of wrong inventory data costs. Re-read the Architect theory on audit trails and data integrity.
A change that's invisible for three days in a production inventory system is a monitoring failure. Contractual advance notice helps but doesn't eliminate the need for your own monitoring.
tasks.why: Third-party model dependencies require continuous accuracy monitoring because model updates (intentional or silent), API changes, and input distribution shift can all silently degrade your system's performance. The minimum viable monitoring: a daily automated run against a fixed golden test set of known-correct extraction pairs; an alert when accuracy drops more than X% from baseline; a rollback plan (previous extraction method, manual process) for when the alert fires. Three days of wrong inventory data in a restaurant context means wrong ordering, potential stockouts or waste, and incorrect financial records.
